chunks.title: Description
chunks.content: Mathematical foundations from discrete mathematics for analyzing computer algorithms, for both correctness and performance; introduction to models of computation, including finite state machines and Turing machines.

chunks.title: Prerequisite
chunks.content: Grade of C or better in MATH 142, MATH 147, MATH 151, or MATH 171

chunks.title: Learning Outcomes
chunks.content:
Upon completion of this course, the learner will be able to:
- Understand the basic principles of logic, proofs, and sets.
- Apply results from discrete mathematics to the analysis of algorithms.
- Produce proofs by induction and apply counting techniques.
- Explain models of computation.

chunks.title: 1. Attendance and Make-up
chunks.content:
If an absence is excused, the instructor will provide the student an opportunity to make up any quiz, exam, or other work that contributes to the final grade or provide a satisfactory alternative by a date agreed upon by the student and instructor. The student is responsible for providing satisfactory evidence to the instructor to substantiate the absence. The reasons absences are considered excused by the university are the following (see Student Rule 7 for details student-rules.tamu.edu/rule07). Failure to notify or document properly may result in an unexcused absence. Falsification of documentation is a violation of the Honor Code.

Other absences may be excused at the instructor's discretion with prior notification and proper documentation. In cases where prior notification is not feasible (e.g., accident or emergency), the student must notify by the end of the second working day after the absence, including explaining why notice could not be sent before the class. Accommodations sought for absences due to the observance of a religious holiday can be sought either before or after the absence but not later than two working days after the absence.

To request an absence, you must fill out and submit a Request of absence form. The instructor will review your request and make a decision.
If your request is approved, you are responsible for covering the material you missed and contacting the instructor to complete any missed assignments.

chunks.title: 2. Submission of Work, Deadline Policy, and Late Submission
chunks.content:
• You must submit all assignments on Gradescope.
• Late assignments are not accepted.
• The homework is due by 11:59 PM on the due date.
• Timed assignments must be submitted within the allocated time.
• Email submissions are not accepted.
• If you have difficulties finishing an assignment, contact the instructor before the deadline.

One-time pass
• You are eligible to receive a one-time waiver for one late homework.
• You can only use the pass once.
• The one-time pass gives you a 24-hour extension past the due date.
• Fill out this form to submit a late homework within 24 hours after the due date.

chunks.title: 3. Grades
chunks.content:
Grades for all the assignments will be released on Gradescope or GitHub and gradually posted on Canvas.

A student can request regrading of any graded material if the student believes that the points assigned are inconsistent with the quality and merits of the submitted work. To request regrading, the student needs to follow the guidelines below:
• Regrade requests must be submitted to the instructor within one week after the graded item is returned to the student. After this time limit, no regrade requests will be honored.
• Regrade requests must be submitted electronically, with reasonable justification and documentation.

chunks.title: 4. Communication rules
chunks.content: Do not send messages on Canvas. The Canvas inbox is not monitored, and your messages will not be read.

chunks.title: Private communication
chunks.content: For private communication, email the course instructor at davidkebo@tamu.edu.

chunks.title: Public communication
chunks.content: Post all questions and comments on the course Discord server. Discord is designed and managed so that you can get help quickly and efficiently from classmates, the TA, and the course instructor.

chunks.title: 6. Collaboration & help
chunks.content:
You are prohibited from collaborating on homework with classmates or other people. Collaborating in this context means copying work from another source and submitting it as your own (plagiarism) or allowing another person to copy your work and submit it as their own (complicity).

We report all violations to the Aggie Honor System Office, and the minimum penalties are:
• A grade of 0 on the assignment
• A grade of F* in the course
• Honor Violation Probation

chunks.title: 6. Collaboration & help (Getting Help)
chunks.content:
You are allowed to discuss the homework on a conceptual level only. The implementation of the solution must be your work.
The right way to get help is to attend my office hours, the TA's office hours, or the help sessions.

chunks.title: 7. Prohibition of help from external websites
chunks.content:
This course assumes that all work submitted by students will be generated by the students themselves. Students should not have another person/entity do the writing of any substantive portion of an assignment for them, which includes hiring a person or a company to write assignments and using artificial intelligence tools like ChatGPT.

Uploading assignments or copying solutions from external websites is a serious violation of the course rules.

chunks.title: 11. Copyright and Acknowledgment
chunks.content: The material used in this course is copyrighted. The material includes all the content provided in the course, including the syllabus, assignments, notes, videos, solutions, etc. You do not have the right to share the material or upload it to other websites.
